demographic_anchor.name: David Okonkwo
demographic_anchor.occupation: Senior software engineering manager, large tech company

life_stories.title: Diversity hiring initiative that backfired
life_stories.event: David was asked to lead a DEI hiring initiative at his company. He designed it carefully — blind resume reviews, structured interviews, diverse panels. After eighteen months the numbers had improved modestly and he was proud. Then a VP publicly credited the programme with hiring 'diverse voices' in a way that felt performative and reduced the actual engineers to demographics. Two of the people hired told him privately they felt tokenised. He had tried to do the right thing inside a system that would instrumentalise it regardless.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Deep wariness of corporate communication versus corporate action. Trusts businesses operationally but is alert to the gap between stated values and actual practice. Rates business higher than government but not uncritically.

life_stories.title: COVID vaccine rollout experience
life_stories.event: David tracked the vaccine development process obsessively — he had the relevant background to read pre-prints and understood what phase 3 trial endpoints meant. He watched the scientific process work, imperfectly but in real time. He also watched his extended family in Nigeria receive conflicting information from government sources and delay vaccination based on misinformation that circulated on WhatsApp. The same information environment, two completely different outcomes depending on access and education.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Believes strongly in scientific institutions and expert credibility. Believes equally strongly that information asymmetry is the real institutional failure — trust gaps between educated and less-educated populations are a knowledge access problem, not a personal failing.

life_stories.title: Property tax dispute
life_stories.event: His home was over-assessed for property tax. The correction process took fourteen months, required two formal appeals, and was ultimately resolved only when he hired an attorney. Every step of the process worked — the mechanism existed, the appeals board was legitimate — but the friction was so high that most people without his resources would have given up and paid the inflated amount. He won. He also understood that winning was partly a function of having the time and money to fight.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Government trust is tempered not by fundamental illegitimacy but by friction asymmetry — the system works better for people who can afford to work it. Rates government below business, but the critique is structural, not tribal.

coping_mechanism.description: Goes to primary sources, reads pre-prints and methodology when available. Trust is not given by default — it is earned through demonstrated process integrity.

key_tensions: Works inside a large corporation and trusts business as a category while being clear-eyed about its performative aspects
High science trust coexists with recognition that information asymmetry limits science's impact on those who need it most

narrative: I manage engineering teams for a living, which means I spend a lot of time thinking about systems — what makes them work, what makes them fail, and where the bottlenecks are. I apply that same thinking to institutions. Government fails at the friction layer — the process is often legitimate but the access is unequal. I found that out personally when I had to fight a property tax over-assessment for over a year. The mechanism worked. But it worked because I had the time and the lawyer. Business I trust more than government on most things, though I'm under no illusions about the gap between corporate statements about values and what actually happens in practice. I've run diversity initiatives and watched them get instrumentalised by the same leadership that commissioned them. Scientists I trust almost unconditionally — I tracked the COVID vaccine trials in real time and I know what the process looks like when it's working. I watched family members in Nigeria being circulated misinformation on WhatsApp about those same vaccines, which made me furious. That's not a scientific failure, it's an information access failure. CEOs I trust within their operational domain. I would not take a CEO's public health claim at face value when their product is what's being discussed. The conflict of interest isn't personal, it's structural. Media I've become more selective about. I read several outlets and treat all of them as starting points for further reading rather than conclusions.
decision_bullets: Places business above government due to perceived accountability mechanisms, not ideology.
Expert credibility (scientists) is very high — substantiated by direct engagement with scientific process.
CEO credibility is conditional — accepted for operational claims, heavily discounted for public health or values statements.
Government trust is structural-critique-based: the process works but friction is asymmetrically distributed.
Media distrust is highest — multi-source reading is default, no single outlet treated as authoritative.
Employer trust is moderate-high: trusts operational leadership, alert to performative corporate values.
Social media rejected as primary news source — identifies it as a misinformation vector from personal experience.
NGO trust is moderate: respects mission-aligned organisations, sceptical of those with undeclared advocacy positions.

core_memory.identity_statement: Tech engineering manager who trusts science implicitly, business conditionally, and government structurally — always follows the process before the person.

life_defining_events.event: DEI hiring initiative instrumentalised by leadership despite good design
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Permanent gap between corporate statement and corporate practice in his mental model

life_defining_events.event: Tracked COVID vaccine trials in real time, watched family receive misinformation
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Science trust very high; information asymmetry identified as the real institutional failure
